We propose a systematic approach for dynamic modeling of multivariate systems, and suggests that many case studies and complex processes could be modeled effectively using this approach. 3. To ensure the optimum structure for the specific application, we optimize the architecture and training algorithm of the two LSTM models using Bayesian optimization for hyperparameter tuning.   ggests that many case studies and complex processes could be modeled effectively using this approach. 3. The rest of the paper is organized as follows: A thorough review of related literature is presented in Section 2. Section 5 presents experimental results of the estimation and future step ahead predictions using the proposed LSTM models. The results are compared and the two models are discussed both from a modeling and plant operation point of view. Finally, Section 6 summarizes this paper. # 2. Related work Modeling of WWTPs has been ongoing for decades with the activated sludge model (ASM) as one of the first successful attempts to model the ASP (Henze et al., 2002). Mechanistic models like the ASM and the subsequent augmented ASM models (ASM2, ASM2d, ASM3) have provided a foundation for many WWTP optimization studies (Hansen et

- Term `plant` at 6413:
  ; Pisa et al., 2019b; Stentoft et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2020). Regarding phosphorus dynamics, Kazadi Mbamba et al. presents a simulation study in which phosphorus is modeled using a mechanistic kinetic model based on equilibrium approach of ion speciation and ion pairing (Kazadi Mbamba et al., 2016). For this study, the benchmark simulation model no. 2 (BSM2) was calibrated in five steps and used to model the case plant. Similar studies have been published, where mechanistic models have been calibrated and utilized for simulation purposes (Solon et al., 2017; Feldman et al., 2017; Kazadi Mbamba et al., 2019; Flores-Alsina et al., 2021). However, the problem remains a major challenge in both academia and industry as the wastewater processes are highly nonlinear, coupled and time-varying dynamic systems containing both physical

- Term `full-scale` at 7438:
  xity of the models, demand for prior knowledge and model calibration before utilization (Dias and Ferreira, 2009). In order to describe coupled biological and chemical processes in the system, the mechanistic models often involve a very large number of state variables. To calibrate and utilize known mechanistic models for WWTPs, the model developer must acquire information obtained from laboratory-scale experiments, full-scale plant data such as those collected by online sensors, and default values from the literature (Stentoft et al., 2019; Kazadi Mbamba et al., 2016; 2019). As these three types of information can be difficult to collect, the developer may be interested in another approach to model the processes of the WWTP. Data-driven modeling (DDM) and system identification is a research area based on computational intellige

- Term `online` at 8066:
  fficult to collect, the developer may be interested in another approach to model the processes of the WWTP. Data-driven modeling (DDM) and system identification is a research area based on computational intelligence (CI) and machinelearning (ML) methods with overlapping contributions from artificial intelligence (AI), soft computing (SC) and data mining (DM) (Solomatine et al., 2008). Recent advancements in DDM and nonlinear system identification provides the developer and the wastewater industry with an alternative to the mechanistic models (Newhart et al., 2019). As a result, DDM has gained acceptability and is increasingly applied to model WWTPs (Husin et al., 2021; Zhao et al., 2020; Newhart et al., 2019; D├╝rrenmatt and Gujer, 2012). Another reason for the recent increase of applied DDM to WWTP may be due to the already exis

  ocus of the present work, is of particular interest given its suitability for both salinity reduction and as a barrier against multiple contaminants [17]. The operation of RO systems, along with their feed pretreatment train, is typically intermittent with respect to the entire system or a segment section (e.g., RO banks) due to multiple reasons including: (a) the need to balance treated water production relative to temporal variability of water consumption [20]; (b) membrane cleaning via feed or permeate flush, and chemical cleaning in place [2123]; and (c) maintenance shutdowns. Intermittent RO plant operation involves the modes of startup, treated water production, shutdown, and flushing (immediately after cessation of production). From an operational viewpoint, it is critical to ensure that product water quality (i.e., RO pe

- Term `forecast` at 4060:
  vel (MCL) and not higher than the secondary MCL (SMCL) (e.g., salinity not above 500 mg/L total dissolved solids (TDS)) [24]. Therefore, when the product water does not meet the required water quality it has to be diverted to discharge, even during short periods, to ensure regulatory compliance. In this regard, operational strategies would benefit from being supported by predictive system operational models to allow forecasting of product water quality throughout the different system's operational modes. Such operational models should consider the various process factors including, but not limited to, water source quality, temperature, membrane type, and system operational parameters [25,26]. Various studies have proposed the use of machine learning (ML) based models to describe and forecast the performance of spiral-wound RO sy

ML models can be utilized to assist in fault detection [36,37], operational control [3840], and forecasting system performance [30].   sequential and time-series data analysis. LSTM is frequently utilized as a powerful analytical instrument for the construction of predictive models, capitalizing on its ability to accommodate long-term dependencies within water quality data. Nonetheless, LSTM models encounter difficulties in preserving and conveying long-term dependencies across prolonged time periods, especially in the context of mid- to long-term forecasting of time-series data. To overcome these limitations, Cho et al. [25] and Sutskever et al. [26] proposed the LSTM Sequence to Sequence (Seq2Seq) model. The LSTM Seq2Seq model consists of an encoder and decoder, utilizing a compressed context vector of past information for learning. This structure enables the flexible configuration for input and output data sequence, thus successfully diminishing the long-te

- Term `time series` at 11842:
  er supply systems. Various previous studies have mentioned that WQ prediction models based on Machine Learning (ML) and Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) are more proactive and effective compared to WQ monitoring through a set point method. The major research contents conducted in this study are as follows: 1) In order to address the long-term dependency issue that occurs in the process of predicting WQ observed as a time series, the study applied LSTM Seq2Seq as the basic model, by referencing previous research results. Univariate WQ observed at the study site was used to develop both LSTM and LSTM Seq2Seq models, and their predictive performance was compared with each other. 2) To improve the learning stability of LSTM Seq2Seq, which can be affected when the prediction interval for wQ extends from shortterm to medium-to-long-te

- Term `time series` at 15360:
  operation. Fig. 1. The architecture of the long short-term memory network. LSTM, with its three additional gate operations, improves long-term dependency learning and addresses the gradient vanishing problems that are inherent shortcomings of RNN. This results in relatively superior performance in short-term predictions. LSTM still faces challenges in maintaining and transmitting long-term dependencies when learning time series data with long-interval patterns, as well as in making mid- to long-term predictions. To address these challenges, Cho et al. [15] and Sutskever et al. [16] proposed the LSTM Seq2Seq model, which incorporates the concept of the Encoder-Decoder architecture. Study procedure In this study, we developed the LSTM Seq2Seq and hybrid water quality prediction models to predict EC and pH based on data collected from the study site, G_WTP. The main research procedures are illustrated in Fig. 4. First, the original data of each WQ is used as the input feature of the LSTM Seq2Seq and hybrid water quality prediction model. Then, the target WQ data was split into 60 %, 20 %, 20 % ratios for Training, Validation, and Testing of each model, as shown in Fig. 4. Therefore, training used 13,566 samples (Jan/01/2019, 00:00-Jul/19/20, 03:00), and 4521 samples were used for validation (Jul/19/2020, 04:00-Jan/ 23/2021, 13:00) and testing (Jan/23/2021, 14:00-Jul/07/30, 23:00), respectively. Second, when considering the batch, the input and output features of the LSTM Seq2Seq

- Term `SCADA` at 1687:
  ing scrupulous control of all processes involved in the intake and processing of water. Currently, this country conducts regular monitoring of a number of wQ parameters such as temperature, pH, dissolved oxygen (DO), electrical conductivity (EC), total organic carbon (TOC), and turbidity [2]. Moreover, WQ data measured by sensors are regularly stored and manages in a database through a telemonitoring platform with a SCADA system. However, quite high seasonal variations of the turbidity in watersheds and frequent algal bloom development create significant problems for water treatment processes. In addition, 58.8 % of water treatment plants and 36.9 % of water supply pipelines have been over 20 years old, raising anxieties about contamination of water quality due to deteriorated facilities that reach their service life [1,3]. Sout

- Term `real-time` at 2383:
  er 20 years old, raising anxieties about contamination of water quality due to deteriorated facilities that reach their service life [1,3]. South Korea has adopted a set point method for WQ monitoring and pollution detection across the entire water supply system, including water sources. This method involves setting legally established WQ criteria for each process as thresholds and issuing a pollution alert when the real-time measured WQ exceeds these thresholds. K. Umberg and S. Allgeier [4] has suggested a set point method for WQ monitoring, which could appropriate approach in respect to online water pollution monitoring due to practical advantages such as low implementation cost. In respect to wQ monitoring, the set point method has many major drawbacks. Initially, sensors placed at strategic locations in the water supply inf

- Term `operator` at 3485:
  shift outliers because of sensor negligence or failure will render the threshold less effective. thirdly, there is the critical issue of false-negative errors, as the method fundamentally will not be able to detect the subtle and early-stage pollution occurring below the threshold [57]. In this context, addressing and overcoming these limitations of the set point method has become a major challenge for designers and operators concerned with South Korea's water supply system. At the same time, this has further highlighted the importance of in-depth analysis and prediction of WQ. Quantitative data processing of measured wQ typically involves statistical analysis. However, because statistical analysis relies on the autocorrelation of time-series data and the correlation between multivariate data, its application can be limited when

[10] applied ANNs, the group method of data handling (GMDH), and support Vector Machines (SVM) to predict WQ in the Tireh River, located in southwestern Iran, with each model demonstrating acceptable accuracy. A.N. Ahmed et al. [11] proposed a method for processing large amounts of nonlinear and non-parametric wQ data using a wavelet de-noising technique combined with a Neuro-Fuzzy Inference System (WDT-ANFIS) for effective water resource management. The introduction of ML and ANNs in WQ analysis and development of prediction models, therefore, has allowed superior predictive models to be developed by accounting for the data nonlinearity effectively, as compared to the classical statistical and

- Term `application` at 10319:
  al. [26] proposed the LSTM Sequence to Sequence (Seq2Seq) model. The LSTM Seq2Seq model consists of an encoder and decoder, utilizing a compressed context vector of past information for learning. This structure enables the flexible configuration for input and output data sequence, thus successfully diminishing the long-term dependency issues arising from mid- to long-term predictions. Hence, LSTM Seq2Seq has seen an application in a wide range of domains regarding time-series prediction, such as language translation and speech recognition. In the context of wQ prediction model development, key studies involving the introduction of LSTM Seq2Seq include J. Bi et al. [27], who applied data preprocessing with the Savitzky-Golay filter and LSTM Seq2Seq for predicting DO and chemical oxygen demand (COD) in rivers. And M. Yuan et al. [

- Term `application` at 11076:
  for predicting DO and chemical oxygen demand (COD) in rivers. And M. Yuan et al. [28] proposed W-Seq2Seq, which combines wavelet transform and LSTM-based Seq2Seq, to improve water quality prediction performance. Similarly, H. Cai et al. [29] utilized the Kalman filter for data preprocessing and LSTM Seq2Seq to predict parameters such as pH and DO in river water. These approaches highlight the ongoing innovation and application of advanced LSTM models to effectively manage and predict water quality issues, serving as a significant environmental monitoring and management avenue. The main objective of this study is to propose a methodology for developing predictive models that assist in effectively monitoring water quality (wQ) and detecting pollution in water supply systems. Study area The target area of this study was the G_Water Treatment Plant (G_WTP) located in Gyeonggi Province, South Korea. The G_WTP utilizes sand filtration processes and has a water treatment capacity of 250,000 m/day. In each process at G_WTP, EC, turbidity, residual chlorine, and pH are measured in real time, and the data is automatically managed using a remote-control system and SCADA. In this study, the LSTM Seq2Seq and hybrid water quality prediction models were developed wi

- Term `real-time` at 45237:
  n the actual and predicted values in mid-to-long-term predictions, and this is particularly noticeable in steps beyond 24 h. This delay in the predictive model's response to real-world changes highlights areas for further improvement in the model's ability to handle longer prediction horizons effectively. # 5. Conclusion The primary objective of this study was to propose a predictive model development approach using real-time WQ observed at WTPs in South Korea, aimed at proactively monitoring water pollution. To achieve this, the LSTM Seq2Seq was applied as the baseline for WQ prediction. Additionally, a hybrid water quality prediction model incorporating ML was proposed to enhance the learning stability of LSTM Seq2Seq in mid-to-long-term predictions and to minimize prediction errors. This study focuses on predicting TMP in a bench-scale MBR by employing advanced regression models such as Lasso, support vector machines, and random forest. The bioreactor is operated using synthetic textile wastewater, with a primary focus on critical sludge parameters. A total of 60 datasets were compiled for training and testing of the models. - Term `training` at 20398:
  and sludge properties (MLSS, PSD, DSVI, B_Car, SOUR) over 20-day cycles post-chemical cleaning. Autocorrelation analysis using the autocorrelation function in R revealed a strong lagone autocorrelation coefficient of 0.745, statistically significant at the 5 % level, indicating substantial temporal dependence in TMP values. We avoided random splitting to prevent data leakage. Instead, we partitioned the data into a training set (first 80 %, 46 observations) and a testing set (last 20 %, 12 observations) to preserve chronological order, ensuring no future data influenced past predictions. Contrary to random sampling, which is unsuitable for temporally correlated data, this method was chosen specifically to respect the strong autocorrelation identified in TMP values (lag-1 autocorrelation coefficient of 0.745, p < 0.05). - Term `split` at 21823:
  roperties with TMP. # 3.1. Random forest regression (RFR) model RFR is an ensemble method that enhances the predictive power of decision trees by averaging the outputs of multiple trees, each trained on different subsets of the data and features. This approach improves accuracy and robustness and reduces overfitting. Each tree is trained on a bootstrapped sample, and a random subset of features is considered at each split. The final prediction is the average of all tree predictions. The out-of-bag (OOB) error, calculated using samples not included in the training of corresponding trees, offers an internal estimate of model performance without a separate validation set. Additionally, the random forest provides measures of feature importance, helping identify the most influential variables in predicting the target, thus clarifying

- Term `training` at 23063:
  dictions from all the trees. # 3.2. Support vector regression (SVR) Support vector machines (SVM), are used for classification and regression [34]. SVR adapts SVM to predict continuous outcomes by fitting a function within a tolerance margin (epsilon) around observed values. Support vectors, or data points outside this margin, shape the regression line. The regularization parameter ŌĆ£CŌĆØ controls the trade-off between training error and model complexity. SVR can employ various kernel functions (linear, polynomial, radial basis) to capture complex, non-linear relationships between input features and the target variable. Lasso regression (L1R) Lasso (Least absolute shrinkage and selection operator) is a linear regression technique that uses L1 regularization to enhance model accuracy and interpretability. By adding a penalty proportional to the sum of the absolute values of coefficients, Lasso can shrink some coefficients to zero, effectively selecting the most predictive features. This results in simpler, more interpretable models and helps prevent overfitting by discouraging overly complex models. T

- Term `training` at 1199:
  ter treatment to ensure environmental sustainability. This study addresses these challenges using a novel deep reinforcement learning approach based on the Soft ActorCritic algorithm, integrated with a custom simulator designed to model the delayed feedback inherent in wastewater treatment plants. The simulator incorporates Long Short-Term Memory networks for accurate multi-step state predictions, enabling realistic training scenarios. To account for the stochastic nature of delays, agents were trained under three delay scenarios: no delay, constant delay, and random delay. The results demonstrate that incorporating random delays into the reinforcement learning framework significantly improves phosphorus removal efficiency while reducing operational costs. Specifically, the delay-aware agent achieved 36% reduction in phosphorus

- Term `training` at 3935:
  23). Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) is a state-of-the-art deep reinforcement learning (DRL) algorithm known for its exceptional sample efficiency and stability in continuous action spaces (Haarnoja et al., 2018). Traditional policy gradient methods, such as Trust Region Policy Optimization (TRPO) (Schulman, 2015) and Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) (Schulman et al., 2017), have advanced DRL by providing stable on-policy training. However, they generally require more samples, as they cannot reuse past experiences efficiently (Lillicrap, 2015). In contrast, SAC is an off-policy algorithm, allowing it to reuse past experiences from a replay buffer, significantly enhancing sample efficiency. SAC effectively balances exploration and exploitation by leveraging a maximum entropy framework, making it especially valuable for complex industri

- Term `future` at 6591:
  rparameter tuning; not as sample</td></tr></table> One significant challenge in applying RL to industrial processes, including WWTPs, is the presence of time delays. Time-delayed systems are prevalent in many industrial settings, and ignoring these delays can lead to suboptimal or unstable control policies (Bouteiller et al., 2020). Incorporating time delays into the RL framework requires advanced methods to predict future states or adapt policies for delayed feedback. Research has shown that DRL algorithms, when appropriately modified, can effectively manage time delays and improve control performance in such systems (Bouteiller et al., 2020). This paper aims to explore the application of the SAC algorithm in the context of WWTP control and optimization, addressing the challenges posed by time delays. The SAC algorithm is parti

- Term `training` at 7147:
  ly manage time delays and improve control performance in such systems (Bouteiller et al., 2020). This paper aims to explore the application of the SAC algorithm in the context of WWTP control and optimization, addressing the challenges posed by time delays. The SAC algorithm is particularly suitable for this application due to its unique advantages. SAC combines maximum entropy reinforcement learning with off-policy training, enabling robust policy learning in stochastic environments (Haarnoja et al., 2018). Its ability to handle continuous action spaces and its efficient exploration-exploitation trade-off make it well-suited for dynamic WWTP operations with time delays. Furthermore, SACŌĆÖs inherent stability and sample efficiency allow it to outperform TRPO and PPO in environments where high-dimensional control and temporal vari

Studies have demonstrated that DRL can optimize aeration control in wastewater treatment plants, leading to significant energy savings while maintaining effluent quality standards (Chen et al., 2021). Despite these successes, challenges such as sample efficiency, safety concerns, and the need for large amounts of data for training persist. Ongoing research addresses these issues by developing more efficient algorithms and integrating DRL with model-based approaches to enhance real-world applicability (Nian et al., 2020). DDPG has been applied to optimize chemical processes, demonstrating its effectiveness in managing high-dimensional control tasks (Panjapornpon et al., 2022). However, DDPG is known to suffer from instability and sensi

- Term `training` at 10953:
  bility (Nian et al., 2020). DDPG has been applied to optimize chemical processes, demonstrating its effectiveness in managing high-dimensional control tasks (Panjapornpon et al., 2022). However, DDPG is known to suffer from instability and sensitivity to hyperparameter tuning, which can hinder its performance in dynamic and uncertain environments (Matheron et al., 2019). On the other hand, the PPO algorithm improves training stability with its clipped surrogate objective function (Schulman et al., 2017). PPO has been employed to process control (Croll et al., 2023) and optimization of pumping systems (Seo et al., 2021) in WWTPs. However, PPOŌĆÖs reliance on fixed clipping parameters may limit its adaptability to highly stochastic processes like those encountered in wastewater treatment plants (Zhang et al., 2024).   on introduces significant variability and challenges in maintaining optimal control, requiring the agent to adapt dynamically to the systemŌĆÖs stochastic behavior. These delays complicate the credit assignment process, as the agent must wait for the effects of prior actions to manifest in the observations before accurately evaluating their impact. This delay increases the complexity of optimizing such systems, as the temporal misalignment between actions and their observed outcomes can suppress causal relationships. To address this challenge, this study incorporates a mechanism that accounts for differing delays in action and observation variables. Introduction Wastewater treatment is a critical process in maintaining environmental sustainability and public health. Traditional control methods often struggle with wastewater treatment plantsŌĆÖ complex, nonlinear, and time-varying nature (WWTP). Machine learning (ML) methods have been used in various industries to model and optimize the systems (Zhang et al., 2023, 2020). Reinforcement learning (RL) which is an ML method, offers a promising alternative by learning control policies through interaction with the environment, thereby adapting to the inherent complexities and uncertainties of the process

- Term `application` at 4358:
  ining. However, they generally require more samples, as they cannot reuse past experiences efficiently (Lillicrap, 2015). In contrast, SAC is an off-policy algorithm, allowing it to reuse past experiences from a replay buffer, significantly enhancing sample efficiency. SAC effectively balances exploration and exploitation by leveraging a maximum entropy framework, making it especially valuable for complex industrial applications with high exploration costs (Haarnoja et al., 2018; Nian et al., 2020). Its ability to handle high-dimensional state and action spaces enables SAC to develop sophisticated control strategies, a crucial feature for wastewater treatment plants, where multiple interacting variables must be managed concurrently (Croll et al., 2023). Introduction The escalating water scarcity and degradation of freshwater ecosystems pose significant threats to the sustainable development of human society (Aldhyani et al., 2020). Wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs) play a prominent role in restoring the connection to clean water supplies by employing a combination of physical, biological, and chemical technologies to remove contaminants from sewage (Dai et al., 2022). As of 2021, China has established the world's second-largest number of WWTPs, following the United States, with a total of 6791 (Guo et al., 2023). The effluent quality and efficiency of WwTPs are i

Quantitative models have been documented for evaluating the impact of physical, chemical, and biological conditions in both influent and effluent on the removal of carbon, nitrogen, and phosphorus in sewage. These established mathematical models play an increasingly crucial role in achieving the targeted effluent quality in bi

- Term `application` at 3595:
  al, and biological conditions in both influent and effluent on the removal of carbon, nitrogen, and phosphorus in sewage. These established mathematical models play an increasingly crucial role in achieving the targeted effluent quality in biological wastewater treatment processes (Calise et al., 2020). These modeling approaches represent important milestones in the development of energy-saving designs for practical applications. They involve the integration of the activated sludge process, decision-making utility, and parameter optimization, contributing to more efficient and sustainable wastewater treatment systems (Bach et al., 2014). Modelling of WWTPs has been undertaken to predict common effluent parameters, encompassing total dissolved solids, nutrient removal, chemical oxygen demand, and various biological processes (Zha

- Term `application` at 4209:
  nt systems (Bach et al., 2014). Modelling of WWTPs has been undertaken to predict common effluent parameters, encompassing total dissolved solids, nutrient removal, chemical oxygen demand, and various biological processes (Zhang et al., 2023). Models designed for WwTPs should be readily adaptable to specific situations and identical treatment technologies encountered in these facilities. Nevertheless, the widespread application of current models in wastewater treatment processes is hindered by the demand for complex chemometric and kinetic parameters (Meuwly, 2021). Tailored mathematical models designed to account for intricate practical variations may compromise result accuracy, thereby presenting significant challenges for practical implementation. To tackle these challenges, automating predictions through machine learning eme

- Term `forecast` at 8676:
  on (production schemes) that might determine the characteristics of the biosolids stream that will have to be handled. To address these limitations, this study presents a data driven approach to assess biosolids dewatering in industrial decanters based on upstream and downstream production information. The proposed analytical pipeline includes a set of tools to identify operational modes, analyze process delays, and forecast polymer dosages combining both numerical and categorical data. The plant under study is the largest industrial WWTP in Northern Europe, in Kalundborg (Denmark), and treats the wastewater produced at two biotech companies (Novozymes and Novo Nordisk). - Term `forecast` at 12867:
  s depending on the upstream production, which can vary substantially. The primary focus in this study is the optimization of flocculant dosage, (in this case an organic polymer) since it was found to be the main control handle for improving dewatering and the one most frequently adjusted by the plant operators. The proposed optimization method is based on finding patterns in operational data and building data-driven forecasting models to predict the polymer dosage and analyzing the developed models for the impact production sites A, B and C have on the predicted polymer dosage. # 2.3.   tep 2. The next step (Step 4) is to study process delays. Indeed, due to the semi-continuous operation of the tanks, a large delay is expected when correlating the upstream with the downstream data. The delay is equivalent to a hydraulic delay, which corresponds to the residence time in storage tanks. This step helps to identify the right determination of the number of past observations (lags) which is important for time series forecasting. Step 5 involves batch detection and characterization to distinguish various switches from one operational model to another. In Step 6, the start time of each batch (after an operation switch) is encoded using time-related features such as year, month, week/day, weekday, hour, minute to account for cyclical and seasonal nature of the input data.   e by controlled mixing of biosolids and flocculant in a defined test. Peeters et al. [9] reported that the laboratory scale was representative of largescale decanters. However, Gnisty et al. [10] found that, while gravity belt thickeners and filter press units could be approximated in the lab, decanter centrifuges could not, since lab tests have the tendency to overestimate the dryness of biosolids compared to their full-scale counterparts. Indeed, the forces inside the decanter will cause flocs to break at a different rate than in a laboratory setup, leading to mixed results when comparing laboratory tests with full-scale performance [11]. In addition, where input solids vary, laboratory methods must be used to continually optimize polymer dose and type. This is required in different work shifts (morning, afternoon, and night).

- Term `application` at 5554:
  ances in the decanter [1214]. These models consider fundamental sedimentation mechanisms. Particle trajectory depends on a few parameters including the particle size and density. Particle separation fundamentals are used to derive separation efficiency [13]. The model by Wang et al. [13] assumes that the solids fraction and flowrate of the feed slurry are constant, which is often not the case in industrial settings. Application of this model identified that solids recovery is impacted by feed rate, solids concentration and particle size distribution [15]. The mentioned mechanistic models require the density and size of the particles as an input. Although the density and size of the particles can be measured outside of the decanter, for biosolids these properties change once the weak structure of the biosolids is exposed to the s

- Term `plant` at 6201:
  input. Although the density and size of the particles can be measured outside of the decanter, for biosolids these properties change once the weak structure of the biosolids is exposed to the shear force in the decanter. Moreover, these properties are influenced by the addition of polymer. Leung [14] developed a mechanistic model for inferring the insitu floc size given the recovery efficiency. However this requires plant data and hence is not a suitable approach for optimization purposes. Instead, the operators require a methodology that can utilize information obtained before the decanter to predict the optimal polymer dosage to achieve sufficient separation. Data-driven methods, on the other hand, are not based on fundamental understanding of underlying mechanisms of the system. Rather they rely on often hidden correlations a

- Term `plant` at 7216:
  ed to mechanistic approaches [17], these models are easier to establish and faster to simulate [18]. In recent years, there has been an increasing number of studies using data driven approaches due to their higher prediction capabilities compared to more traditional mechanistic counterparts. Bello et al. [19] and Jayaweera & Aziz [20] could predict surface charge and pH for a coagulation process in a water treatment plant. Hong et al. [21] predicted both dewatering performance and dosage optimization at a wastewater treatment plant. More recently, hybrid models (also called gray box models) which combine a mechanistic and data-driven approach have been also suggested [22]. In this hybrid approach, a data-driven model can compensate for uncaptured physical behavior in the mechanistic model. In a model by Menesklou et al. [23], me

This property is critical in drinking water safety, as underestimating FIB concentration poses a direct risk to public health, potentially leading to inadequate treatment response. By contrast, a slight overprediction results in more conservative safety measures, which are preferable in this context. # 2.4. Prediction interval PIs provide a range within which a future observation is expected to fall, given a specified confidence. Unlike confidence intervals, which quantify the uncertainty of estimated parameters (e.g., the mean of a population), PIs capture uncertainty at the individual predictions level, incorporating both model uncertainty and intrinsic data variability. This makes PIs particularly valuable in environmental and microbial risk assessment, where uncertainty

Introduction Ensuring a safe drinking water supply is a critical global public health priority. Water quality in drinking water treatment plants (DWTPs) is monitored by collecting data from both laboratory analyses and real-time sensor measurements. This data encompasses a wide range of chemical, physical, and microbiological parameters that reflect the current state of water quality. While initially used for routine monitoring, the growing volume of collected data offers far more potential when leveraged through machine learning (ML) techniques. These

- Term `application` at 6605:
  has gained significant attention as a promising method for uncertainty quantification. It offers distinct advantages for generating prediction intervals because it is a distribution-free framework that provides coverage guarantees. Unlike many other methods, CP does not require any assumptions about the data distribution, making it highly flexible and broadly applicable. Despite its capabilities CP has seen limited application in the water field. For instance, in groundwater applications, MAPIE algorithms have been integrated with Gradient Boosting Decision Trees (GBDT) to estimate heavy metal concentrations [24]. In surface and recreational waters, conformalized quantile regression (CQR) has been used to generate prediction intervals for Enterococci concentrations [25]. Moreover, machine learning models have produced predictio

- Term `application` at 8133:
  nto a robust predictive model. In most recent benchmarking studies, GBDT algorithms have demonstrated a superior predictive performance over other models due their ability to handle complex relationships in data, handle missing values and provide robust predictions [29,30]. Given these strengths, GBDT models are especially promising for critical steps in drinking water treatment where predictions are vital. One such application is Quantitative Microbial Risk Assessment (QMRA). QMRA is a methodology used to estimate the health risks posed by pathogenic microorganisms, from environmental concentrations to exposure. This approach is increasingly valuable in managing water reuse related health risks [31]. For instance, recent work has proved that coupling hydrological simulations with QMRA can be used to enable an effective risk man

  n layer, a key structure of the algorithm, is composed of nodes, and each node calculates the output value for the next step using a non-linear function called the activation function. An increase in the number of hidden layers enables more complicated calculations or predictions from the input variables. However, the increase in the number of hidden layers is limited as it causes problems such as overfitting to the training data and vanishing gradient during the optimization of the models. DL was developed by overcoming the limitations of ANN. The DL algorithm uses an alternative activation function, such as a rectified linear unit instead of the conventional sigmoid function, which reduces the gradient vanishing problem. - Term `online` at 7039:
  ar and widely used GBDT models (Chen and Guestrin, 2016; Zhang et al., 2018). An important consideration in utilizing advanced ML algorithms (e.g., ensemble ML models and DL models) for water quality prediction is that such models rely less on the physical or chemical relationships between independent input variables and target variables for the prediction, focusing instead on enabling the estimation of complicated nonlinear relationships between input and target variables. However, the increased complexity of the model structure may reduce the model's interpretability.   imulation configured for nitrogen removal.18 As a simulation, the BSM1 benefits from well-defined influent patterns, a simple process configuration, and precise performance evaluation metrics, which enable the direct comparison of different control strategies. 19 The BSM1 is also well suited to the evaluation of model-free RL agents, which almost exclusively require a computer simulation in order to reach sufficient training requirements before being transferred to a physical environment, known as the simulation-to-real (Sim2Real) transfer.16,24,25 While several studies have evaluated the application of RL control optimization to the BSM1, much of the existing literature has been limited to evaluating RL optimization of the standard BSM1 control strategy: adjusting the internal mixed liquor recycle (IMLR) rate to maintain a solu

- Term `rolling` at 5938:
  erational costs, each focused on a single class of RL algorithms under a single set of input features and did not evaluate more than n = 1 agents for each scenario to demonstrate consistency of agent performance. To provide a more systematic and comprehensive evaluation of RL for BSM1 control optimization, this study evaluated four types of RL algorithms for their ability to optimize BSM1 system performance when controlling either only DO or controlling both DO and IMLR, under several sets of input features.   demonstrated the relative efficacy of different types of RL agents for wastewater treatment control optimization, identified how changes to input features affect agent performance, and evaluated several successful agents for their ability to cope with three challenge test scenarios for noisy influent data and sensor failure. To accomplish this evaluation, this study also presents a novel RL environment that enables training of RL agents from existing Python libraries on SUMO process models. # MATERIALS AND METHODS Benchmark Simulation Model No. 1. The BSM1 simulation consists of an influent, five completely stirred tank reactors (CSTRs), of which CSTRs 1 and 2 are anoxic and CSTRs 3-5 are aerobic, a final clarifier, internal mixed liquor recycle (IMRL), return activated sludge (RAS), and waste activated sludge (WAS)(Figure S1).

- Term `training` at 7233:
  liquor recycle (IMRL), return activated sludge (RAS), and waste activated sludge (WAS)(Figure S1). The BSM1 has several control variables, including the DO set points and IMLR flow rate, which can be varied in order to minimize energy use while maintaining effluent quality, particularly for nitrogen species. Further information on the BSM1, including calibration, can be found in the Supporting Information. RL Agent Training Environment. - Term `operator` at 2540:
  ocesses that occur during treatment. Another type of control strategy that has gained attention from researchers in recent years is that of machine-learning (ML) control, which is not directly based on domain knowledge but on the empirical relationships among variables that can be leveraged through modern data science. In particular, reinforcement learning (RL) stands out for its ability to perform better than human operators for certain high-dimensional, complex decision-making problems.610 Unlike other ML techniques, online RL algorithms that are considered here do not learn from static historical data sets but by directly exploring different actions based on a set of observation variables, often referred to as features, to maximize a specified reward. Several recent review papers have been published evaluating various facets

- Term `implementation` at 2964:

Several recent review papers have been published evaluating various facets and implementations of RL algorithms.11-16 While RL control optimization in the context of wastewater treatment has almost exclusively been confined to academic research, the ability of RL to generate process efficiency improvements makes it of significant interest to the industry moving forward.17 Among both domain and ML control optimization strategies, a common method for the evaluation of wastewater treatment control

The resulting efluent quality and operational parameters maintained by the agent control were evaluated for overall treatment plant performance. Plant performance was based on an overall operational cost index (OCI), which included aeration energy (AE), pumping energy (PE), mixing energy (ME), and sludge production (SP), an effluent quality index (EQI), based on kg of pollutants discharged per day, and several effluent limits: total nitrogen <18 mg/L, COD <100 mg/L, Soluble NHx <4 mg/L, TSS <30 mg/L, and BOD5 <10 mg/L.   h changes can be difficult or impossible to assess visually in real time or with a univariate monitoring strategy, such as setting fixed upper and lower control limits. To improve fault detection efficiency and performance, a fault detection approach capable of capturing changes between variables is needed. Statistical process monitoring (SPM) is a data-driven approach to real-time fault detection that compares new, testing observations with fault-free observations collected during a previously observed training period. Unlike other data-driven fault detection approaches, such as classificationbased methods,1,2 SPM requires no prior knowledge of the faults that may appear, and an SPM model can be built even if few or no faults have been previously observed. Prior knowledge of faults is unnecessary because SPM functions by identi

- Term `testing` at 2008:
  free observations collected during a previously observed training period. Unlike other data-driven fault detection approaches, such as classificationbased methods,1,2 SPM requires no prior knowledge of the faults that may appear, and an SPM model can be built even if few or no faults have been previously observed. Prior knowledge of faults is unnecessary because SPM functions by identifying significant departures of testing observations from the normal operating behavior during the training period.3,4 A popular and easy-to-implement approach to fault detection with SPM is to apply univariate control charts on each variable of interest. A wide variety of univariate methods exist, such as Shewhart,5,6 cumulative sum (CUsUM),7,8 and eoenlly m control charts. A discussion of various univariate SPM methods is given by Woodall and Mon

- Term `time series` at 5266:
  ne learning (ML) model is fit to each monitoring variable using the explanatory variables as model predictors. Then, model residuals are obtained, which are the differences between the model predicted values and the actual values of the monitoring variable. Some work has been done using multiple regression to detrend monitoring variables prior to monitoring.21,22 It is also common to monitor the residuals from ML or time series models, but these methods do not distinguish between explanatory and monitoring variables in the process.23-28 Instead, they predict each monitoring variable using previous values or other process variables as predictors. Separating process variables into "explanatory" or "monitoring" reduces the number of variables being monitored directly and leads to a more straightforward interpretation of any OC sign

  ods mplemented for real-time usage over long periods of time, and short-term performance may not always translate to long-term detection ability. Changes in influent quality, equipment, or operation are expected to occur over time and can affect model performance. To maintain long-term monitoring performance, adjustments to SPM and MSPM methods are needed. To adjust univariate SPM methods for long-term monitoring, a rolling training window can be used to automatically update upper and lower control limits to account for nonstationarity (e.g., seasonal changes in means). The size of the rolling window; the number of consecutive observations classified as OC needed to trigger an alarm; and the selection of control limits to achieve a prespecified number of false positives are unique to the process. MSPM PCAbased methods used without any adjustments are referred to as static PCA, but many adjustments to PCA have been proposed to improve fault detection performance.37 These include dynamic PCA,19,38 adaptive PCA39,40 and adaptive-dynamic PCA (AD-PCA).3,41,42 Dynamic PCA accounts for autocorrelation in the data by including lags of detrended monitoring variables, adaptive PCA uses a rolling window approach to account for nonstationarity, and AD-PCA incorporates both adjustments, making it suited for monitoring multivariate autocorrelated data over long periods of time.   an and raise an alarm if the value exceeds an upper or lower threshold value. The threshold is calculated as a predetermined number of standard deviations away from the monitoring variable's IC mean or outside a specified confidence interval, both of which are based on the assumption of normality of the monitoring variable. In this work, an adaptive Shewhart chart is used to address nonstationarity by implementing a rolling window of time to recalculate the mean and standard deviation after each day of IC observations. A 99.5% confidence interval, which is approximately 2.8 standard deviations from the mean, is used to determine the Shewhart control limit(s). Additionally, alarms are issued only after 5 consecutive threshold exceedances occur in order to minimize the number of false alarms due to measurement error or noise. Furt

Hering, and Kathryn B. Newhart\* Cite This: ACS EST Engg. 2024, 4, 1492-1506 # Read Online B mreulvprousanl e-ter iluiauri # 1.INTRODUCTION Advancements in water treatment include improved technologies, such as ultrafiltration (UF), and improved process monitoring, with the addition of instrumentation and advanced controls. The various sensors and analyzers in the treatment process have led to an increased availability of data, potentially allowing for better identification of faults in the process.

- Term `operator` at 662:
  fication of faults in the process. However, large amounts of data can present a challenge for operators. Changes to the treatment process, such as UF membrane fouling or undesired changes in influent or efluent quality, must be monitored properly to maintain filtration efficiency and to avoid accidental discharge of potentially harmful contaminants. The primary method for process monitoring in water treatment is for operators to visually examine plotted trends or values of individual process variables to determine if they differ significantly from normal operating conditions. This method of manual fault detection can be especially difficult when changes are small, occur gradually over time, or affect multiple variables simultaneously. Furthermore, meaningful changes in the relationships between process variables can be overlooke

- Term `deploy` at 16710:
  . The MSPM approach in this work uses PCA-based methods, which are especially useful when the number of process variables is large. Once the training period, monitoring variables, and explanatory variables are identified, we detrend the monitoring variables by fitting a statistical or ML model. PCA is then applied to the residuals from each detrending model to compute monitoring statistics and their thresholds. When deployed, new observations are detrended, and PCA is applied using the previously fitted models. Then, the monitoring statistics are calculated and compared to the previously defined thresholds. This results in the classification of each new observation as IC or OC (i.e., monitoring statistic is below or above the threshold, respectively). Methods for detrending are described in Section 2.3.1, the MSPM model with AD-

We select four regression methods for detrending: adaptive lasso, k-nearest neighbors (KNN), random forest (RF), and extreme gradient boosting (XGBoost).   o discover sophisticated structures from high-dimensional data.21 DL surpasses traditional ML in enhanced feature extraction, recognition, and the capability of self-learning and selfcompletion. In solving the problem o modeling predictive models, the long short-term memory (LSTM) network stands out among multiple DL algorithms and has been successfully applied in the fields of financial market prediction,23 weather forecasting,24 and so on. The recurrent and gate structure determines the power of the LSTM in capturing long-term dependencies from a dynamic time series,25-27 which matches the characteristics of WWTPs. Nevertheless, the structure also makes its black-box nature obvious, and its internal principles and mechanisms are unknown, which has led to controversy.28 And explainable DL is now an emerging research front in ma

- Term `time series` at 5770:
  t in many fields, and there have been attempts in the field of wastewater treatment process modeling. Hwangbo et al. developed a deep neural network for process modeling and considered global sensitivity analysis based on variance decomposition to identify the key parameters.20 But few studies have applied explainable methods to understand the prediction process of LSTM due to the obstacle caused by the multivariate time series (MTS) of inputs. Consequently, it is a meaningful and novel attempt to explain how MTS inputs act on LSTM outputs to improve model explainability and overall performance. This study presented an explainable data-driven model based on the LSTM algorithm and Shapley additive explanations (SHAP) analysis to predict key effluent indicators (COD, TN, and TPin a full-scale WWTP.First, multiple long-term monitor

  Abundant operational data provide extensive opportunities for the development of machine learning (ML) and deep learning (DL) models. However, the coupling and time lag among the features exacerbate the black-box nature of such models, hindering their application in WWTPs. In this study, we construct a DL model using a long short-term memory (LSTM) algorithm capable of accurately predicting the effluent quality in a full-scale WWTP with finely tuned hyperparameters and rationally chosen input features. Comprehensive model explanation based on Shapley additive explanations (SHAP) is implemented to clarify the contributions of M A results. - Term `DSS` at 2217:
  th Water Transmission (WTS) and Water Distribution (WDS) Systems. Te former, generally characterized by long pipelines with large diameters, have few or no connections to the consumers property, while the latter are characterized by shorter pipelines with smaller diameters and several connections to properties to feed consumers. WTSs facilitate the transfer of water volumes to consumption centres (towns or cities). WDSs, also called Water Distribution Networks (WDNs), because of their networked structure, transfer water to end consumers. Terefore, the issue of water quality with respect to the contamination and disinfection for people health is mainly related to WDNs. Water quality modelling in WDNs deals with the evaluation of water age, water trace and transport of the reactant substances considering the decay due to chemical

- Term `application` at 7130:
  n integrated with machine learning. John Koza was the pioneer who developed the paradigm of genetic programming, showing in a book4 the possibility of creating machines that program themselves to solve problems postulated by humans. Genetic programming integrates machine learning, in a wider sense with respect to the original studies, with evolutionary optimization in an original way. Symbolic modelling is a specifc application of KozaŌĆÖs paradigm to obtain models by means of the integration of machine learning and, e.g. genetic algorithms5,6 as an evolutionary optimization technique, in form of symbolic formulas for models that can be evaluated as such by the expert. EPR is then a genetic programming strategy coding in an original way the problem to obtain formulas for models and involving multi-objective analysis through geneti

  ng objectives. Te chlorine reactions have been classically modelled using frst order kinetics10, but several second order models have also been proposed11,12. Te second order model with a single reactant species has been applied by authors such as13ŌĆō15. Other second order models with multiple competing species have also been introduced12,16. According to11, the initial disinfection that occurs in the water treatment plant may be more appropriately modelled with a second order model since a rapid initial loss takes place. Conversely, when the disinfected waters reach the distribution system, the decay rates are reduced and both frst and second order kinetics may apply.   can return model formulas with the best trade-of between complexity and accuracy to data, so that the analyst can take the decision on the best model looking at the Pareto front and their symbolic/explicit (understandable) structure. ### Validation snippets

- Term `forecast` at 1903:
  l of ML for modeling urban water systems, whether for storm-, waste-, or potable water systems. Notwithstanding the increased applications and potential benefits of common ML models, the robustness and potential liability for predicting system dynamics receive less examination. Common ML models for water system modeling are typically formulated as supervised ML problems. Given observed data, ML models are trained to forecast quantities of interest such as chemical oxygen demand (COD), biochemical oxygen demand (BOD), total phosphorus (TP), TN, and suspended sediment concentration (SSC) (Guo et al. 2015; Huang et al. 2021; Sundui et al. 2021; Viet and Jang 2023). In such common ML models based on supervised learning, three main obstacles potentially hinder their predictive capability: (1) common ML models are largely data-driven.

- Term `training` at 3241:
  mon ML models for water system modeling, while different in the detailed model implementation [e.g., ANN, random forest (RF), decision tree (DT), SVM, k-nearest neighbors (KNN) are commonly formulated as supervised learning (Huang et al. 2021). As demonstrated by Mallat (2016), "Supervised learning is a high-dimensional interpolation problem." Therefore, common ML models require large and diverse data sets for model training. However, distinct from conventional applications of ML models in social networks and computer vision, data acquisition for water systems at this time is more challenging and less economical (Jia et al. 2021). Except for selected parameters monitored with online sensors in real-time (e.g., dissolved oxygen, turbidity, pressure, ammonia), basic and critical parameters still primarily rely on discrete samples

- Term `training` at 3827:
  e challenging and less economical (Jia et al. 2021). Except for selected parameters monitored with online sensors in real-time (e.g., dissolved oxygen, turbidity, pressure, ammonia), basic and critical parameters still primarily rely on discrete samples and laboratory analysis such as SSC, particle size distribution, TP, TN, and metals (other inorganic and organic chemicals notwithstanding). With a limited database, training and development of a common ML can result in poor model generalizability and can be less robust (Newhart et al. 2019). As a type of recurrent neural network (RNN), LSTM is specifically developed for modeling time-dependent data. As illustrated in Fig. 1(c), LSTM combines features from the previous time and current time for prediction (indicated by the vertical connection lines between LSTM units in each layer). The temporal relationship within the input feature is considered through hidden states and cell states. Moreover, compared with vanilla RNN, LSTM overcomes the issue of a vanishing gradient in learning long-term dependencies by the novel architecture design of cell states and gating mechanisms (Hochreiter and Schmidhuber 1997). As suggested by the name, LSTM is capable of capturing long- and short-term dependencies of th

As suggested by the name, LSTM is capable of capturing long- and short-term dependencies of the data. The training data layout with LSTM adopted here is identical to the previous time-lagged mapping method. Since LSTM is often applied to predict labels at one future time instant, a stacked "many to one" LSTM structure is illustrated in Fig. 1(c). # Physics-Informed Neural Networks for UOPs In contrast with common ML models, SciML hybridizes physics principles and domain knowledge with data. In recent years, ML techniques have been increasingly applied for modeling natural and engineered water systems (Pyo et al. 2020; Valencia et al. 2021; Huang et al. 2021; Li et al. 2021a, b; Abdeldayem et al. 2022; Liu et al. 2022; Sundui et al. 2021; Trueman et al. 2022; Ly et al. 2022). Zhi et al. (2021) implemented a long short-term memory (LSTM) model for predicting dissolved oxygen in rivers at a continental scale in the United States (US). Such research demonstrates the applicability and potential of ML for modeling urban water systems, whether for storm-, waste-, or potable water systems. Notwithstanding the increased applications and potential benefits of common ML models, the robustness and potential liability for predicting system dynamics receive less examination. Common ML models for water system modeling are typically formulated as supervised ML problems.   ough novel ML architecture. In SciML, ML models reflect data-driven learning yet are not "black boxes" and are deeply embedded with prior knowledge of the underlying system physics (Ghattas and Willcox 2021). In the last few years, several SciML frameworks and ecosystems have been developed for dynamic system modeling such as universal differential equations (UDEs) (Rackauckas et al. 2020), sparse identification of nonlinear dynamics (Kaptanoglu et al. 2022), neural ordinary differential equations (neural ODEs) (Chen et al. 2018), and physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) (Raissi et al. 2019). By incorporating domain knowledge, SciML can significantly improve predictive capability and robustness compared with common ML methods. Further, SciML has also demonstrated higher efficacy for many inverse problems that challenge conve

- Term `application` at 6088:
  e, SciML can significantly improve predictive capability and robustness compared with common ML methods. Further, SciML has also demonstrated higher efficacy for many inverse problems that challenge conventional mechanistic models (differential equations), such as model parameter estimations and discovery of hidden dynamics (Yazdani et al. 2020). This study leverages recent advancements in SciML and presents a novel application for PINNs to common UOPs in urban water treatment, whether for storm-, waste-, or potable water treatment. The specific objectives of this study are to generalize and summarize common ML methods and models for water system modeling and to examine and compare the predictive capability of common ML and SciML models (i.e., PINNs) for common UOPs: (1) a CSTR; (2) an activated sludge reactor; (3) a fixed bed a

  ural Networks for UOPs In contrast with common ML models, SciML hybridizes physics principles and domain knowledge with data. Specifically, PINNs introduced by Raissi et al. (2019) integrate the prior domain knowledge into ANN by posing constraints on the loss function in ANN training. Other SciML approaches such as neural ODEs (Chen et al. 2018) have been recently adopted (Quaghebeur et al. 2022). Herein, the PINNs implementation directly facilitates integration for ODE and PDE systems (or mixed PDE-ODE systems). The capability of accommodating PDEs is necessary for modeling timevarying and spatially dependent water systems (demonstrated in the later section). Water utilities collect a vast number of discrete samples from their customer taps to measure various water quality parameters and regulatory compliance. While this is a huge effort and creates large datasets it is still sparse (spatially and temporally) data compared to the size and complexity of DWDS. Prior work on discolouration that applied machine learning (ML) methodologies to this kind of data indicated that these methods have the potential for both informing the causes of discolouration and estimating the risk of iron exceedance in the DWDS [2, 3]. All the calculations were performed in MATLAB 2019b. # 4.1. Comparison of ML models CRF is an ensemble learning method for classification which combines a collection of decision trees. It improves the prediction by introducing a split on a random subset of features, i.e., it randomly selects observations and variables to build multiple decision trees and then averages all the trees. This makes it robust in dealing with non-linear data with outliers. CSVM uses classification algorithms to find a hyperplane in a multi-dimensional space (due to multiple variables) that distinctly classifies the data points into two groups. It has the advantages

- Term `training` at 12588:
  s into two groups. It has the advantages of high speed and good performance with datasets where a limited number of observations is available. CBT-RUSBoost is a type of decision trees suitable for imbalanced datasets. It uses boosting algorithms combined with a random under-sampling method to reduce the size of majority class and thus improve the performance of the model. The three ML models are compared in terms of training (with the data of 2009 to 2018) and prediction of unseen data (data of 2019), and their performance is summarized in Table 2. CSVM shows very poor performance. It calculated most of the events as non-exceedance (high TNR and low TPR in training, and TPR of zero in validation) due to the highly unbalanced data. Therefore, it is not considered as a suitable method for the present application. RUSBoost and CRF

  mates for best setup. Input parameters, p1 to p4, are DMA yearly average of iron, turbidity, three-day HPC, and high priority dead ends per DMA, respectively. # 4.6. Risk of iron exceedance CRF grows a collection of individual decision trees. Each tree produces a class prediction ("votes" for a class). Then the forest chooses the class having the most votes over all the individual trees. There are two classes in our application, ŌĆśEŌĆÖ and ŌĆśNŌĆÖ, denoting exceedance and non-exceedance events. Thus, if the fraction of trees that vote for an event to be in the class ŌĆśEŌĆÖ is above 0.5, that event is considered as an exceedance event, and if it is below 0.5, the event is a non-exceedance. Probability of a class is then obtained by counting the fraction of trees in the forest that voted for that certain class. The probability calculated fo

  # Forecasting of Wastewater Treatment Plant Key Features Using Deep Learning-Based Models: A Case Study TUOYUAN CHENG 1, FOUZI HARROU 2, (Member, IEEE), FARID KADRI3, YING SUN 2, AND TOROVE LEIKNES 1 1Division of Biological and Environmental Science and Engineering (BESE), Water Desalination and Reuse Center, King Abdullah University of Science and Technology (KAUST), Thuwal 23955-6900, Saudi Arabia 2Statistics Program

- Term `forecast` at 513:
  Abdullah University of Science and Technology (KAUST), Thuwal 23955-6900, Saudi Arabia 3Aeroline and Customer Services, Agence 1024, Sopra Steria Group, 31770 Colomiers, France Corresponding author: Fouzi Harrou (fouzi.harrou@kaust.edu.sa) This work was supported by the King Abdullah University of Science and Technology (KAUST), Office of Sponsored Research (OSR) under Award OSR-2019-CRG7-3800. ABSTRACT The accurate forecast of wastewater treatment plant (WWTP) key features can comprehend and predict the plant behavior to support process design and controls, improve system reliability, reduce operational costs, and endorse optimization of overall performances. Deep learning technologies as proven data-driven soft-sensors should be developed for WWTP applications to tackle the process of non-linearity and the dynamic nature of en

- Term `forecast` at 1015:
  ict the plant behavior to support process design and controls, improve system reliability, reduce operational costs, and endorse optimization of overall performances. Deep learning technologies as proven data-driven soft-sensors should be developed for WWTP applications to tackle the process of non-linearity and the dynamic nature of environmental data. This study adopts deep learning-based models as soft-sensors to forecast WWTP key features, such as influent flow, influent temperature, influent biochemical oxygen demand (BOD), effluent chloride, effluent BOD, and power consumption. We constructed six deep learning models derived from long short-term memory (LSTM) and gated recurrent unit (GRU), namely traditional LSTM and GRU, the exponentially smoothed LSTM, and the adaptive version of LSTM and smoothed LSTM. The employment o

- Term `forecast` at 1519:
  ochemical oxygen demand (BOD), effluent chloride, effluent BOD, and power consumption. We constructed six deep learning models derived from long short-term memory (LSTM) and gated recurrent unit (GRU), namely traditional LSTM and GRU, the exponentially smoothed LSTM, and the adaptive version of LSTM and smoothed LSTM. The employment of a smoothed LSTM technique is expected to reduce the outlier effect and to improve forecasting accuracy. Meanwhile, the usage of adaptive deep models will enhance the capabilities of the LSTM to quickly and accurately follow the trend of future data. We compared the performance of these models with Bi-directional LSTM (BiLSTM) and the seasonal decomposition using local regression. The historical records from a coastal municipal WWTP in Saudi Arabia are used to verify the investigated modelsŌĆÖ effect

ABSTRACT In this paper, we propose a deep reinforcement learning (DRL) based predictive control scheme for reducing the energy consumption and energy cost of pumping systems in wastewater treatment plants (WWTP), in which the pumps are operated in a binary mode, using on/off signals. As global energy consumption increases, the efficient operation of energy-intensive facilities has also become important. A WWTP in Busan, Republic of Korea is used as the target of this study. - Term `online` at 13941:
  ze of the time window. Considering the problem of immediacy in the sewage treatment problem, we concentrate on the prediction of short periods and anomaly detection. Therefore, we set n = 1, i.e., to predict a one-time data unit after the time window. The N-BEATS architecture differs from existing architectures in several ways. First, instead of viewing forecasting as a sequence-by-sequence problem, we view it as a nonlinear multiple regression problem. Thus, the architecture of the basic building block (see Fig. 2, left) is a fully connected nonlinear regressor that accepts the history of a time series and outputs multiple points within the forecast horizon. Second, most existing time series architectures are relatively shallow (e.g., one to five LSTM layers). Therefore, we use the residual principle to stack many layers on top

- Term `implementation` at 14833:
  rpose, the underlying blocking path predicts future outputs and their contributions to the input decomposition, which we call backtracking. The literature [48] shows that this principle can be used to effectively stack on the order of 100 layers, resulting in a very expressive model with good generalization capabilities. Another advantage of the architecture is its simplicity, demonstrated at both the conceptual and implementation levels. Conceptually, we can consider each fully connected layer as a multiple linear regression blocking the path, followed by a ReLu [49] nonlinearity. Thus, an N-BEATS can be seen as a multiple regression repeated and intertwined with a nonlinearity. Conceptual simplicity translates into implementation simplicity. FIGURE 2. N-BEATS model. ABSTRACT The echo state network (ESN) is a cutting-edge reservoir computing technique designed to handle time-dependent data, making it highly effective for addressing time series prediction tasks. ESN inherits the more precise design of standard neural networks and the relatively simple learning process and has a strong computing capacity for solving nonlinear problems. It can disseminate low-dimensional information cues to high-dimensional areas enabling extracting data. However, this study has proven that not all reservoir output dimensions directly impact model generalization.

Moreover, empirical findings on predicting a significant water quality parameter from the wastewater treatment process (WWTP) dataset demonstrate that the proposed ensemble of BIGSA models performs very well in real-world scenarios. - Term `time series` at 2846:

This field creates holistic algorithms based on traditional research to predict future values. Timeseries estimates exhibit the ability to predict the future by analyzing the history [2]. The economics [3], energy [4], electromechanical systems [5], and engineering [6], [7] are largely depend on predicting. Also, time series estimation is a complex problem since it is a nonlinear dynamic issue. A superior model of functional prediction competencies should be established to boost time series forecasting [8]. Appropriate computer methods were developed to enhance prediction accuracy [9]. However, no universal standards exist for determining the optimal solution to a specific concern [10]. Artificial neural networks (ANNs) have

Automated monitoring systems are costly and require regular maintenance and calibration. Remote sensing involves using satellite and aerial imagery to monitor water quality from a distance. Remote sensing covers larger areas and provides spatial and temporal data but may not provide detailed information on specific water quality parameters. # C. IoT-based Water Monitoring The Internet of Things (IoT) refers to a network with connected hardware and software. IoT-based water monitoring is a type of automated monitoring system that uses internet-connected sensors to collect and transmit real-time data on water quality [6ŌłÆ8]. - Term `application` at 2863:
  h to improving water quality is to release environmental probiotics such as polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) biofilters [1,2]. The PVA biofilter is made of a water-soluble synthetic polymer by encapsulating and immobilizing beneficial bacteria that break down organic matter in wastewater. The biofilter allows the bacteria to grow and thrive, treating wastewater stably and effectively. PVA biofilters have been used in various applications, including in the treatment of sewage and industrial wastewater. We measured the effectiveness of these PVA biofilters by monitoring water quality. There are three common methods of water quality monitoring: manual sampling, automated monitoring systems, and remote sensing [3ŌłÆ5]. Manual sampling is usually time-consuming and mainly used in remote areas without telecommunication signals. Automated # A. Wa

- Term `real-time` at 4971:
  velop appropriate strategies to protect water resources and ensure their safe use. There are several methods for water monitoring, including manual sampling and laboratory analysis, automated monitoring systems, and remote sensing. Manual sampling involves physical water sample collection at a specific location and sending them to a laboratory for analysis. This method is generally time-consuming and may not provide real-time data. Automated monitoring systems use sensors and devices to continuously measure and record water parameters such as temperature, pH, dissolved oxygen, and conductivity. The data collected by these systems are transmitted in realtime to a central database for analysis and interpretation. Automated monitoring systems are costly and require regular maintenance and calibration. Remote sensing involves using

- Term `real-time` at 5863:
  y from a distance. Remote sensing covers larger areas and provides spatial and temporal data but may not provide detailed information on specific water quality parameters. # C. IoT-based Water Monitoring The Internet of Things (IoT) refers to a network with connected hardware and software. IoT-based water monitoring is a type of automated monitoring system that uses internet-connected sensors to collect and transmit real-time data on water quality [6ŌłÆ8]. IoT-based systems monitor a wide range of parameters, including temperature, pH, dissolved oxygen, turbidity, and conductivity. They also alarm and alert when water quality parameters exceed predetermined thresholds. The cost of IoT-based water monitoring systems varies depending on the type and number of sensors used, the frequency of data transmission, and the level of data an

- Term `application` at 6467:
  nd alert when water quality parameters exceed predetermined thresholds. The cost of IoT-based water monitoring systems varies depending on the type and number of sensors used, the frequency of data transmission, and the level of data analysis required. However, as IoT technology becomes prevalent and accessible, the cost of these systems is expected to decrease, making them more widely available for water monitoring applications. Based on the installation of sensors and communication devices, we distinguish IoT-based monitoring systems into four categories. ML algorithms learn from large datasets and identify patterns and relationships between various water quality parameters for predictions based on those relationships. One application of ML in water monitoring is to predict the occurrence of harmful algal blooms (HABs). HABs can release toxins that are harmful to aquatic organisms and animals in the water and humans. Another application of ML in water monitoring is to detect and identify contaminants in water.   t University of Salamanca Spain srevolla@usal.es AbstractŌĆö In this work, a model-free Reinforcement Learning method is implemented to control the aeration in the nitrification process in wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs). Controlling real complex non-linear processes is challenging due to model mismatch between the model used in the controller and the real process. Model-based control strategies can fail in a real implementation due to modeling errors. WWTPs include multivariable, nonlinear, and complex biological processes, which makes it difficult to obtain adequate models to represent the dynamics of the process under all possible conditions. For this reason, a model-free reinforcement learning agent that takes advantage of Transfer Learning is selected. This datadriven procedure is trained to deal with the trade-off betwee

- Term `plant` at 1242:
  itions. For this reason, a model-free reinforcement learning agent that takes advantage of Transfer Learning is selected. This datadriven procedure is trained to deal with the trade-off between effluent quality and operating costs. The results demonstrate that model-free RL can reach sub-optimal solutions through a data-driven procedure. Keywords ŌĆö Deep Reinforcement Learning, Transfer Learning, Wastewater Treatment Plants, Intelligent Control. # I. INTRODUCTION Wastewater Treatment Plants (WWTP) [13] [14] stand out for the complex non-linear, and uncertain biological processes that are part of it. In particular, the Active Sludge Process (ASP) [6], represented by recognized ASM models, is used to design and develop control systems such as model-based predictive control (MPC) [7] [15] [16]. However, the process variability could

- Term `control system` at 1721:
  reatment Plants (WWTP) [13] [14] stand out for the complex non-linear, and uncertain biological processes that are part of it. In particular, the Active Sludge Process (ASP) [6], represented by recognized ASM models, is used to design and develop control systems such as model-based predictive control (MPC) [7] [15] [16]. However, the process variability could lead to modeling errors in some conditions. Consequently, control systems designed under these simulation models may fail in real implementation due to unexpected behavior of the microorganisms, the uncertainty of the biological process, temperature changes, disturbance in the input influents, and volume increase effects, among others. In this paper, the application of Machine Learning techniques focuses on the employment of artificial neural networks to model real complex

- Term `plant` at 5045:

It has mainly been used to solve the optimization of indexes linked to Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) indicators [8], and trade-offs between quality effluent and operation cost [9] [10]. In this work, the activated sludge nitrification process is considered to have the most significant influence on effluent quality and operation cost in wastewater treatment plants. In this oxygen-dependent process, oxygen removes ammonia. Ammonia is an essential factor in effluent quality due to an inverse relation with nitrates, another important pollution factor. In contrast, dissolved oxygen is the most crucial economic factor in operating costs. All these phenomena are represented by ASM models. In this work, we employ the Policy Gradient algorithm [4] to train a control agent to de
