KEYWORDS: prediction, artificial neural networks (ANNs), auto regressive integrated moving average (ARIMA). # INTRODUCTION Inadequate management of a Waste Water Treatment Plant may cause serious environmental and public health problems, as its effluent when discharged into a receiving water body can cause or spread various diseases to human beings. Operation of a wastewater treatment plant is often affected by a variety of physical, chemical, and biological factors. In order to follow the treatment plant performance during the operation, effluent measurements would not be sufficient. Predicting any of these parameters, depending upon the influent water quality, will help the operator to control the system and to take necessary precautions before any problem comes up. Modeling a waste water treatm

- Term `plant` at 2048:
  t is often affected by a variety of physical, chemical, and biological factors. In order to follow the treatment plant performance during the operation, effluent measurements would not be sufficient. Predicting any of these parameters, depending upon the influent water quality, will help the operator to control the system and to take necessary precautions before any problem comes up. Modeling a waste water treatment plant is considered a difficult task due to complexity of the treatment processes. A better control of a system can be achieved through developing a mathematical technique for predicting plant performance based on past observations of certain parameters. The complex physical, biological and chemical processes involved in wastewater treatment may exhibit non-linear behaviors which cannot be described by linear mathema

- Term `plant` at 3035:
  est practices for conserving the quality of water. Most of these models need several different input data which are not easily accessible and make it a very expensive and timeconsuming process. ANN has been observed to be a suitable approach for water quality modeling (Chen et al., 2003; Jan-Tai et al., 2006). In addition, ANNs provide different advantages over traditional modeling approach of a wastewater treatment plant. For instance, when ANNs are applied to prediction of wastewater treatment plant performance task they will result in reduction of cost for undertaking laboratory tests. The models were built using the Times Series Forecasting System tool of the R software packages. # Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average Model (ARIMA) Box and Jenkins (1976) developed a practical approach to construct ARIMA models, which have fundamental effects on the applications of time series analysis and forecasting. Their methodology includes the following three iterations: model identification, parameter estimation and diagnostic testing. The autocorrelation function (ACF) and partial autocorrelation function (PACF) of the sampled data are used as the basic tools to identify the order of the best ARIMA model. For the models obtained, diagnostics tests are performed using (a)

- Term `application` at 9027:
  al., 2009). All inputs and outputs are normalized between 0 and 1 in artificial neural network software. A proper process of data normalization and defacement is required before and after the execution of the program. The best and simplest way to normalize is to divide it by the maximum and after the execution of the program, multiply the result by the same amount. There are several neural network models for various applications available in research, but the ANN considered in this study is a fully connected multilayer perceptron (MLP), a network composed of neurons which consists of three layers of neurons: (1) an input layer; (2) a hidden layer, and (3) output layer. As shown in Figure 3, although ARIMA models vary with the range, the model predictions are not adequate. This is due to the limitation of the linear modeling algorithm in the ARIMA model which is unsatisfactory in identifying and predicting nonlinear time series of water quality parameters. # Artificial Neural Network (ANN) As described above, network training is done using a resilient back propagation with weight backtracking algorithm. A sigmoid function is used as the transfer function in both the hidden layer and output layer due to its suitable application.   # AI-driven multivariate time series forecasting of transmembrane pressure in ultrafiltration: A stationarity-aware approach Dongju Kim a , Jong Pil Yun a,b , Taehan Lee c , Daewoong Jung d , Hong-In Won a, a Manufacturing AI Research Center, Korea Institute of Industrial Technology, Incheon 21999, Republic of Korea b Graduate School of Advanced Imaging Science, Multimedia & Film, Chung-Ang University, Seoul 06974, Republic of Korea c Gradua

- Term `forecast` at 505:
  cience and Technology, Pohang 37673, Republic of Korea d Department of Nanomechatronics Engineering, College of Nanoscience & Nanotechnology, Pusan National University, Busan 46241, Republic of Korea # A R T I C L E I N F O Editor: Soroush Abolfathi Keywords: Ultrafiltration (UF) Transmembrane pressure (TMP) Autoregressive forecasting Stationarity analysis AI-assisted operational diagnosis # A B S T R A C T Accurate forecasting of transmembrane pressure (TMP) is essential for effective membrane fouling control and maintenance scheduling in ultrafiltration (UF) systems. This study proposes a stationarity-aware, artificial intelligence-assisted framework that enables long-horizon TMP prediction based on operational data. The framework incorporates autoregressive deep learning modelsŌĆöLong Short-Term Memory (LSTM) and Gated Recurren

- Term `forecast` at 1162:
  ork that enables long-horizon TMP prediction based on operational data. The framework incorporates autoregressive deep learning modelsŌĆöLong Short-Term Memory (LSTM) and Gated Recurrent Unit ŌĆöcombined with stationarity diagnostics using the Augmented Dickey-Fuller test and first-order differencing. Non-stationarity in process variables such as permeate flux and pressure was identified as a key challenge that degrades forecasting performance. Applying differencing to stabilize the input data led to substantial improvements in model accuracy. Specifically, the R2 value of the LSTM model increased from ŌłÆ 4.625 to 0.623 after preprocessing, representing an improvement of 5.25 points. In the final model, the GRU-based predictor achieved an R2 value of 0.890, demonstrating high predictive accuracy. The models were also validated on an

- Term `forecast` at 1755:
  creased from ŌłÆ 4.625 to 0.623 after preprocessing, representing an improvement of 5.25 points. In the final model, the GRU-based predictor achieved an R2 value of 0.890, demonstrating high predictive accuracy. The models were also validated on an independent UF system operating under moderately elevated fouling conditions, demonstrating strong generalization and robustness. The proposed framework enables data-driven forecasting using only routine plant sensor data, without the need for additional instrumentation. This makes it well-suited for integration into existing monitoring systems. By improving TMP prediction accuracy and enabling proactive maintenance, the method offers practical value for long-term UF operation, particularly in reducing unnecessary cleaning cycles and enhancing operational stability. Moreover, the method

- Term `forecast` at 3955:
  owerful tools for modeling and predicting the nonlinear and dynamic behaviors of membrane fouling. Their ability to handle real-time and complex problem modeling offers significant advantages in both water and wastewater treatment applications [2]. Numerous studies have demonstrated the successful application of AI/ML models to address challenges such as performance prediction, fouling detection, cleaning efficiency forecasting, and process optimization. For instance, artificial neural networks (ANNs) and extreme gradient boosting (XGBoost) have been utilized for process optimization and effluent quality prediction [3], while linear models, knearest neighbors (KNN), and ANNs have been applied to predict lift force, transmembrane pressure (TMP), and total resistance [4,5]. In the context of fouling prediction and control, feedfor

- Term `forecast` at 4455:
  (ANNs) and extreme gradient boosting (XGBoost) have been utilized for process optimization and effluent quality prediction [3], while linear models, knearest neighbors (KNN), and ANNs have been applied to predict lift force, transmembrane pressure (TMP), and total resistance [4,5]. In the context of fouling prediction and control, feedforward neural networks and long short-term memory (LSTM) models have been used to forecast fouling in anaerobic membrane bioreactors (AnMBRs) [6], while ANNs have also been employed to predict cleaning performance [7]. Other notable studies include the use of one-dimensional convolutional neural networks (ODCNNs) for protein fouling prediction [8], and integrated models combining LSTM with non-autoregressive multiresolution imputation (NAOMI) for TMP and energy consumption forecasting in seawater

- Term `training` at 6744:
  een reported [23]. Despite the progress made, several significant research gaps and limitations hinder the effective application of AI models to real-world industrial membrane process data. First, data collected from operational facilities often exhibit noise, missing values, outliers, and nonstationarity due to varying operational conditions, making it difficult to construct high-quality datasets suitable for model training. These challenges include data scarcity [8] and issues related to missing data [9]. As a result, operators frequently rely on empirical judgment, repeating procedures without objective decision support. While the importance of data preprocessing is acknowledged in prior work [6], comprehensive integration of process-specific domain knowledgeŌĆösuch as variable selection, valve-state tagging, removal of statist

- Term `forecast` at 7502:
  main knowledgeŌĆösuch as variable selection, valve-state tagging, removal of statistical outliers and transitional periodsŌĆöis rarely discussed in detail. In particular, there is a lack of systematic strategies for quantitatively assessing and stabilizing the non-stationarity of membrane process variables. Second, accurate prediction of membrane fouling requires not only short-term trend detection but also long-horizon forecasting capabilities. Although deep learning models such as LSTM and gated recurrent units (GRUs) have been applied to time series prediction ([6,9,16ŌĆō18]), studies focusing on long-term prediction using short-term input sequences with optimized multivariate autoregressive inputs remain limited. Such capabilities are essential for enabling real-time monitoring and proactive maintenance. Third, the robustness and

- Term `forecast` at 9103:

This integration is vital for ensuring that AI-generated predictions can be understood, trusted, and used effectively in operational decision-making. To address these research gaps, this study proposes an AI-based predictive framework that significantly enhances the long-horizon forecasting capability for membrane fouling in water treatment processes. Specifically, the proposed approach incorporates domaininformed variable selection and advanced data preprocessing techniques to build high-quality training datasets, including methods for handling the non-stationarity of membrane variables. Moreover, multivariate autoregressive input structures are applied to LSTM and GRU models to enable accu

- Term `time series` at 10953:
  prediction model, as well as the input-output variables. The data preprocessing steps, including outlier filtering and removal of anomalous operation segments, are discussed, along with statistical analysis results of data properties and performance metrics used to evaluate model accuracy. Section 4 presents a comparative evaluation of model performance, focusing on the impact of preprocessing and differences among time series forecasting algorithms. To assess the scalability of the proposed models, additional experiments were conducted using data obtained under harsher operational conditions from UF processes. - Term `application` at 2810:
  des in existing treatment facilities. # 1. Introduction Membrane-based processes have firmly established themselves as core technologies in the field of water treatment, owing to their ability to produce high-quality permeate and enable efficient resource recovery. Technologies such as ultrafiltration (UF), reverse osmosis (RO), and membrane bioreactors (MBRs) have demonstrated exceptional performance across various applications, including wastewater treatment, seawater desalination, and potable water production. Despite their advantages, one of the most critical challenges in membrane operations is the unpredictable and complex phenomenon of membrane fouling [1]. Fouling leads to a decline in membrane permeability, increased energy and chemical consumption due to frequent cleaning, elevated operational costs, and ultimately com

- Term `online` at 3582:
  to frequent cleaning, elevated operational costs, and ultimately compromises process efficiency and system stability. Therefore, accurately predicting and effectively managing membrane fouling is essential for ensuring sustainable and cost-effective membrane operations. In recent years, artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) technologies have emerged as powerful tools for modeling and predicting the nonlinear and dynamic behaviors of membrane fouling. Their ability to handle real-time and complex problem modeling offers significant advantages in both water and wastewater treatment applications [2]. Numerous studies have demonstrated the successful application of AI/ML models to address challenges such as performance prediction, fouling detection, cleaning efficiency forecasting, and process optimization. For inst

- Term `plant` at 4898:
  erobic membrane bioreactors (AnMBRs) [6], while ANNs have also been employed to predict cleaning performance [7]. Other notable studies include the use of one-dimensional convolutional neural networks (ODCNNs) for protein fouling prediction [8], and integrated models combining LSTM with non-autoregressive multiresolution imputation (NAOMI) for TMP and energy consumption forecasting in seawater reverse osmosis (SWRO) plants [9]. Wavelet-based classifiers for fault detection in dynamic UF processes [10], and self-organizing maps (SOMs) for fouling analysis [11] have also been proposed. Further applications include deep neural networks for modeling flux decline in nanofiltration (NF) and RO membranes [12], ANNs with response surface methodology (RSM) for UF fouling in olive oil wastewater [13], and ML models for fouling and perform

- Term `full-scale` at 5731:
  performance prediction in textile wastewater AnMBRs [14]. Studies have also targeted reversible fouling prediction in MBRs using neural networks [15], hybrid modeling of RO systems via recurrent neural networks and long short-term memory [16], integrated data-driven coupled with adsorption model for fouling prediction in RO systems [17]. AI models such as random forest (RF), ANN, and LSTM have been widely adopted in full-scale wastewater treatment plants for fouling prediction [18]. Comprehensive reviews on ANN-based modeling in wastewater applications [19], as well as the principles, methodologies, and applications of ML in MBR research [20], further underscore the broad potential of AI/ML in the water sector. In addition to these, certain studies have examined specific process parameters and mechanisms such as the importance o

- Term `application` at 6441:
  er sector. In addition to these, certain studies have examined specific process parameters and mechanisms such as the importance of critical flux and backwashing intervals [21], and sulfate-assisted coagulation for fouling mitigation [22]. A critical review of MBR fouling modeling frameworks has also been reported [23]. Despite the progress made, several significant research gaps and limitations hinder the effective application of AI models to real-world industrial membrane process data. First, data collected from operational facilities often exhibit noise, missing values, outliers, and nonstationarity due to varying operational conditions, making it difficult to construct high-quality datasets suitable for model training. These challenges include data scarcity [8] and issues related to missing data [9]. As a result, operators f

- Term `forecast` at 12794:
  iable data for modeling, resulting in six models tailored to each of the membrane blocks. The CIP is typically performed once TMP reaches 100 kPa. Currently, there is no early warning system to alert operators when this threshold has been reached, requiring frequent manual monitoring of TMP to determine an appropriate time to conduct a CIP. A previous intern of the utility investigated machine learning approaches to forecast TMP and found that a random forest (RF) model yielded the lowest prediction error. Building on that work, this study applies a regression-based RF approach to predict how SF will change in the next week, providing operators with an estimate of future SF with ample time (seven days) for CIP scheduling. The model uses current SF and SF from the previous week as inputs to predict the change in SF that will occu

- Term `full-scale` at 11499:
  re, May, 2025 Ultrafiltration (UF) membrane operation is strongly affected by fouling, where dissolved and suspended water quality constituents interact with each other and the membrane surface, leading to pore blockage. As a result, a fouled membrane loses filtration efficiency, reducing the volume of treated water produced and increasing operation and maintenance costs associated with membrane cleaning. At a Dutch full-scale facility in North Holland, fouling is indicated by both an increase in the amount of pressure required across the membrane, known as transmembrane pressure (TMP), and a decrease in permeability, represented by specific flux (SF). The facility performs hydraulic backwashing - both with and without chemical addition- occurs on a fixed frequency to scour readily removable foulants from the membrane surface. H

- Term `operator` at 12574:
  membrane is soaked in two stages of chemicals for several hours to attack the fouling that remains after backwashing. The UF system consists of eight membrane blocks (i.e., groups of membranes), with six of them having viable data for modeling, resulting in six models tailored to each of the membrane blocks. The CIP is typically performed once TMP reaches 100 kPa. Currently, there is no early warning system to alert operators when this threshold has been reached, requiring frequent manual monitoring of TMP to determine an appropriate time to conduct a CIP. A previous intern of the utility investigated machine learning approaches to forecast TMP and found that a random forest (RF) model yielded the lowest prediction error. Building on that work, this study applies a regression-based RF approach to predict how SF will change in th

- Term `operator` at 13017:
  eshold has been reached, requiring frequent manual monitoring of TMP to determine an appropriate time to conduct a CIP. A previous intern of the utility investigated machine learning approaches to forecast TMP and found that a random forest (RF) model yielded the lowest prediction error. Building on that work, this study applies a regression-based RF approach to predict how SF will change in the next week, providing operators with an estimate of future SF with ample time (seven days) for CIP scheduling. The model uses current SF and SF from the previous week as inputs to predict the change in SF that will occur in the next week. Operational input data was collected from online UF data collectors, while water quality data was collected from both UF influent data collectors and from laboratory measurements. The resulting ŌĆśBlock Mo

Operational input data was collected from online UF data collectors, while water quality data was collected from both UF influent data collectors and from laboratory measurements. The resulting ŌĆśBlock ModelŌĆÖ for each of the six membrane blocks achieved R2 values exceeding 60% and mean square error (MSE) values below 3.20E-8. A combined ŌĆśUniversal ModelŌĆÖ achieved an R2 of 66%. Operators may consult this Universal Model to anticipate changes in SF and proactively plan CIP procedures.   ifferent data, increasing variability between decision trees to improve the overall stability of the final prediction from all trees (Team Gyata, 2023) Chemically enhanced backwashing (CEBW): in membrane filtration, where backwashing of a membrane occurs automatically with a fixed frequency and duration; a chemical addition is added to provide a more aggressive cleaning approach to flush out foulant material. At the pilot and full-scale facilities, this chemical is sodium hypochlorite Classification: a type of prediction task where the output is a class label that can most likely be assigned to the input. - Term `operator` at 17760:
  h determines the path to the next node. The input continues to meet split points and follows a path until it comes to the final node, providing the output. In this way, a CART divides inputs based on multiple decisions (Browniee, 2020b). See below for an example of a CART/decision tree Cleaning in place (CIP): in membrane filtration, the most rigorous cleaning regime occurring manually on demand executed by facility operators, with duration between 2 to 4 hours and frequency left to operator discretion. Frequency, duration, chemicals used, and TMP threshold are site specific and depend on the water quality.
